coredata.dc:title: Lemon peel-based fluorescent carbon quantum dots as a functional filler in polyvinyl alcohol-based films for active packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Jayakumar, Aswathy
Radoor, Sabarish
Shin, Gye Hwa
Kim, Jun Tae
coredata.dc:description: Carbonation of agro-waste into high-value-added products is becoming a promising strategy for producing functional active materials and for reducing landfills. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are of great attention due to their non-toxic, antibacterial, antioxidant, fluorescent, and other functional properties. The current study demonstrated the hydrothermal synthesis of dried lemon peel-based carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) and their incorporation in polyvinyl alcohol for developing active films. The developed LCQDs exhibit excellent fluorescence, antioxidant, and antibacterial activities against S. aureus, B. cereus, S. enterica, L. monocytogenes, and E. coli. Interestingly, as confirmed by FE-SEM and FTIR, LCQDs showed excellent compatibility with PVA-based films. The mechanical and water vapor barrier properties were improved by adding 3% LCQDs to the PVA film, which also resulted in improved UVC (99.9%), UVB (99.9%) and UVA (99.1%) blocking effects. Additionally, PVA film containing 3% LCQDs showed 98.9% radical scavenging activity by ABTS method and was 100% effective against S. aureus and S. enterica. Therefore, 3% LCQDs-incorporated PVA films could have excellent potential as active food packaging materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonation of agro-waste into high-value-added products is becoming a promising strategy for producing functional active materials and for reducing landfills. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are of great attention due to their non-toxic, antibacterial, antioxidant, fluorescent, and other functional properties. The current study demonstrated the hydrothermal synthesis of dried lemon peel-based carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) and their incorporation in polyvinyl alcohol for developing active films. The developed LCQDs exhibit excellent fluorescence, antioxidant, and antibacterial activities against S. aureus, B. cereus, S. enterica, L. monocytogenes, and E. coli. Interestingly, as confirmed by FE-SEM and FTIR, LCQDs showed excellent compatibility with PVA-based films. The mechanical and water vapor barrier properties were improved by adding 3% LCQDs to the PVA film, which also resulted in improved UVC (99.9%), UVB (99.9%) and UVA (99.1%) blocking effects. Additionally, PVA film containing 3% LCQDs showed 98.9% radical scavenging activity by ABTS method and was 100% effective against S. aureus and S. enterica. Therefore, 3% LCQDs-incorporated PVA films could have excellent potential as active food packaging materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage is the major loss associated with the food industry. Recently, the United States Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO) estimated that around 1.3 billion tons of food is misused yearly (Nile et al., 2020). About 40%−50% of fruits and vegetables followed by fish (35%), cereals (30%) and dairy and meat commodities (20%) are damaged by microbial pathogens (Motelica et al., 2020). Inefficiencies in post-harvest storage technologies, wastage due to insufficient transport facilities, and consumers themselves contribute to food loss (Murray et al., 2017). Among various losses, contamination by microbial pathogens is one of the major problems associated with pre-and post-harvest storage. Microbial contamination can lead to the shortened shelf life of foods and altered food quality, and cause a great chance of food-borne diseases (Zhang et al., 2021).

The packaging industry plays a major role in sustaining the quality of foodstuffs. While traditional food preservation methods include freezing, drying, fermentation, heating, and salting, antimicrobial packaging aims to integrate antimicrobial agents into packaging materials that completely inhibit the growth of microbial pathogens (Malhotra et al., 2015). These agents may be incorporated directly into food or integrated into the packaging materials. Several studies are currently reporting the development of active food packaging materials utilizing existing antimicrobial agents. The drawbacks associated with synthetic preservatives, combined with consumer demand for safer ingredients with extended shelf life have paved the way to explore the application of natural antimicrobials. Commonly studied natural antimicrobials include essential oil, polysaccharides, proteins, enzymes, lipids, and other substances including nanomaterials of various origins (Lee et al., 2019; Oun et al., 2022a; Riahi et al., 2023; Satriaji et al., 2020). Antimicrobials from plant products are considered safe and have been reported to improve the shelf life of packed foods (Papadochristopoulos et al., 2021).

Among the natural materials, agro-waste is gaining great attention as additive raw materials for the development of active and intelligent packing materials due to their economic, environmental, and technological advantages. Citrus peel waste is the most common by-product generated during fruit processing and production, accounting for 60% of all processed fruits. These by-products have been reported to contain high amounts of proteins, lipids, starch, micronutrients, dietary fibres, and other bioactive components (Wedamulla et al., 2022). Particularly, lemon (Citrus limon L.) is the third most used citrus fruit after oranges and mandarins. Lemon peel constitutes approximately 50%− 65% of total waste and contains many active ingredients such as polyphenols (ferulic, p-coumaric, and sinapic acids), flavonoids (flavanones, flavanols, and flavones), vitamin C, and essential oils (Huang et al., 2021; Papoutsis et al., 2016). Among these, polyphenols are phytochemicals with a wide range of biological activities and health benefits. Many studies have shown that these compounds have antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, anti-cancer, anti-ageing and neuroprotective effects (Bae et al., 2022; Farghadani and Naidu, 2023; Rani et al., 2022). Therefore, it is used in a variety of applications including cosmetics, pharmaceutics, packaging, and synthetic fuel production (Bhattacharya et al., 2023; Harikrishnan et al., 2020; Jeong et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2017; Suri et al., 2022). Immediately after processing, lemon peels commonly end up in landfills, water bodies, or incinerated. However, there are concerns about the continued disposal of these by-products due to environmental issues related to soil degradation (Mahawar et al., 2019; Zema et al., 2018). A possible reason for this is that these active constituents of lemon peel may have antimicrobial effects on beneficial microorganisms in the soil as well as other species in the environment (water bodies) (Mahawar et al., 2019; Maqbool et al., 2023) and may lead to oxygen depletion of the water. Hence, the utilization of agro-waste such as lemon peel has great potential to develop into multifunctional fillers for polymer films. As an upcycling strategy, the carbonization of lemon peel can have significant potential for developing active and eco-friendly materials.

Recent studies have shown that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could be used as active mediators in food-packing applications (Ezati et al., 2023; Khan et al., 2024; Wen et al., 2023a, 2023b). Hence, the development of CQDs from lemon peel (by-product) could be a sustainable way to develop active environmentally friendly fillers for active packaging. CQDs are a novel category of zero-dimensional nanoforms with size range of less than 10 nm (Das et al., 2018; Roy et al., 2022). They are superior to other organic and inorganic nanomaterials due to their relative non-toxicity, cost-effectiveness, good solubility, high photoluminescence and the occurrence of functional groups, large surface area, and excellent biocompatibility. CQDs can be synthesized very easily from all organic molecules, natural products, and bioresources. Moreover, CQDs can be functionalized with various polar functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine. The major techniques used for CQD development are hydrothermal treatment, ultrasonic method, microwave-assisted synthesis, pyrolysis, and electrochemical oxidation (Jiang et al., 2012). This has led to extensive research in the field of CQDs and various applications such as food packaging, metal ion sensing, photocatalysis, medical diagnosis, and drug delivery (Başoğlu et al., 2020; Riahi et al., 2022). Several studies have shown that the integration of these CQDs into packaging materials can expand the functions of polymer-based films and coatings. In most cases, the film functions such as mechanical strength, thermal stability, gas, water vapor, light barrier, antioxidant, and antimicrobial have been improved by incorporation with CQDs. For example, gelatin films incorporating carbon dots from grape leaves were reported to have improved mechanical, UV barrier and antioxidant properties (Khoshkalampour et al., 2023). Additionally, excellent antioxidant, antimicrobial and UV barrier properties were reported for chitosan/gelatin-based films containing green tea-derived CQDs (Khan et al., 2023b). Another study used PVA/CQDs-based films to extend the shelf life of banana, jujube and fried meatballs (Zhao et al., 2022). The structural, mechanical, and biodegradable properties of polycaprolactone-based films were improved by rosehip fruit-based CQDs (Mindivan and Göktaş, 2023). This has increased their demand for use alone or in combination with other biopolymer or synthetic polymers of interest.

To the best of our knowledge, there is no report of developing CQDs with antioxidant, UV blocking, and antimicrobial properties from dried lemon peel and incorporating them into PVA-based films for developing active materials. Active films are now widely developed because of their promise in reducing microbial spoilage and maintaining the quality of food. Therefore, the main objective of this study was to develop a sustainable PVA-based multifunctional packaging material using CQDs extracted from dried lemon peel (LCQDs). The developed LCQDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Further microstructural and functional properties such as water barrier, mechanical strength, UV blocking, antioxidant, and antibacterial effectiveness of PVA-LCQDs-based films were analyzed.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with a molecular weight of 146,000–186,000 Da (99% hydrolyzed) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO USA). Lemon was procured from the market in Seoul, Korea, and lemon peel powder was prepared by grinding after drying the lemon peel.

LCQDs were prepared from dried lemon peel using a hydrothermal method (Ezati and Rhim, 2022). Briefly, 1 g of dried lemon peel powder was weighed, dissolved in deionized water (50 mL), and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined steel reactor and incubated at 200 °C for 6 h. The remaining supernatant was filtered through nylon filter (pore size: 0.22 µm). The morphological characteristics of LCQDs were analyzed using HR-TEM (JEM2100F, Jeol Ltd., Japan). The chemical properties of LCQDs were analyzed with an FTIR spectrophotometer (ATR PRO 4X, Japan). The fluorescence features of LCQDs were analyzed using fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, F-7100, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial and antioxidant activity of LCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial activity of LCQDs was analyzed against S. aureus, B. cereus, S. enterica, L. monocytogenes, and E. coli by well diffusion method. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of LCQDs were analyzed according to the guidelines of Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute. The tolerance level of pathogens to LCQDS was analyzed as per a previous report (Jayakumar et al., 2019). The antioxidant potential of LCQDs was evaluated using ABTS radical scavenging assay according to a previous methodology (Oun et al., 2022b).

PVA films were developed by the addition of LCQDs using a solvent-casting method (Mathew et al., 2019a). A neat PVA film was developed by dissolving 6 g of PVA in 200 mL of distilled water (DH2O) and stirred at 80 °C for 4 h. LCQDs incorporated composite films were developed by mixing various weight percentages of LCQDs (1%, 2%, and 3% based on PVA) to a PVA solution. The filmogenic solution obtained was then continuously stirred for 1 h and cast and dried at 25 °C for 3 days. Then, all films were taken and placed in humidity compartment for 48 h at 25 °C before their characterization.

The color of the surface of composites was measured using Chroma meter (Konica Minolta, CR-400, Japan). The difference in total color (ΔE) of the PVA/LCQDs films was calculated as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural and chemical characterization of films
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross-sectional properties of the composites were analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan). Chemical structures and functional groups present in the composites were analyzed by ATR-FTIR spectrophotometry in the range of 4000–650 cm−1 with 32 scans of 4 cm−1. The crystalline features of the LCQDs and films were studied by X-ray Diffractometer (DMAX-2500, Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) with a scan range of 2θ = 5–80° at 40 kV, 300 mA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical and water barrier functions of films
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanical features of the samples were analyzed by Universal Testing Machine (UTM, Zwick 2010TN, GmbH & Co. KG, Germany) as per ASTM D 882–88 standards. The cross-head speed was 50 mm/min and the gauge length used was 50 mm. The water contact angle (WCA) of the composites was analyzed by Contact Angle Analyzer (Phoneix 150, Surface Electro-Optics, Korea) (Radoor et al., 2021). The solubility of the composites was analyzed by drying 2 × 2 cm2 film samples for 24 h in hot air oven at 60°. The initial dry weight of the composite samples was noted as Mi. Film samples were placed in 30 mL of DH2O for 24 h. Further, the samples were taken and the final dry weight (Mf) of the films was analyzed by drying the sample at 100 °C for 24 h (Mathew et al., 2019b). The equation used for analyzing the film solubility percentage is given below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV blocking, antioxidant, and antimicrobial effectiveness of films
ce:section.ce:para: The UV blocking ability of the films was evaluated using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (200–800 nm). The transmittance at UVC (250 nm), UVB (300 nm), UVA (350 nm), and visible (550 nm) were recorded as per the previous report (Alaş et al., 2022). The antioxidant potential of the films was analyzed using the ABTS method. Briefly, 50 mg of samples were placed in ABTS solution (10 mL) and positioned in dark for 60 min. The absorbance at 734 nm was measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The antimicrobial effectiveness of the films was evaluated against Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella enterica according to a previous report (Tammina and Rhim, 2023). The prepared culture medium was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h and diluted to 105-106 CFU/mL. Further, 200 mg of film was added to 20 mL broth and incubated at 37 °C in a shaking incubator. After incubation for 6 h, 50 μL of culture medium was inoculated into agar plates. The bacterial reduction percentage was calculated by the colony count method.

LCQDs were successfully synthesized by the carbonization of dried lemon peel powder using a hydrothermal method. The size and morphology of LCQDs were analyzed using TEM ( Fig. 1a). The LCQDs exhibited a size range of 3 to 6 nm, and their average particle size was 5 ± 0.6 nm. The most prominent behavior of CQDs is photoluminescence at various excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm. (He et al., 2018; Nguyen et al., 2022). LCQDs exhibit blue fluorescence when irradiated with 365 nm and transparent under UV light. This may be due to strong photon emission under UV excitation (Yuan et al., 2019). The fluorescence center of CQDs is usually related to the presence of carbon core states, surface defects, and the presence of functional groups (Guo et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2023). The LCQDs exhibit emission characteristics as a function of wavelength and are shown in Fig. 1b. The PL emission wavelength of LCQDs reached a maximum of 400 nm when they were excited at 330 nm. Similar observations were previously reported for green tea-derived CQDs functionally applied to real-time monitoring of shrimp freshness (Khan et al., 2023b). The excitation-dependent PL of LCQDs can be attributed to quantum confinement effect and other factors such as physical and chemical properties of CQDs (Khan et al., 2023a). However, the exact mechanism of the excitation-dependent emission properties is still not fully known.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and antioxidant activity of LCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the hydrothermally synthesized LCQDs showed excellent antibacterial activity by the well diffusion method. The maximum inhibition zone was obtained for S. aureus (27 mm) followed by S. enterica, E. coli, B. cereus, and L. monocytogenes ( Fig. 2 a). For further studies, S. aureus and S. enterica were selected because of the maximum zone of inhibition. The MIC value of LCQDs against S. aureus and S. enterica was 100 μg/μL and their MBC value was 50 μg/mL. The tolerance ratio of S. aureus and S. enterica was found to be 2. A compound is said to be bacteriostatic when the MIC/MBC ratio is greater than 16. A compound is said to be bactericidal when the MIC/MBC value is less than 4 (Davis, 2018). The bactericidal potential of LCQDs was confirmed to be less than the MIC/MBC value of 2 which can be used more safely. CQDs derived from Citrus medica fruit, Azadirachta indica, and Brassica oleracea extract have previously been reported for their bacteriostatic and bactericidal potential (Gedda et al., 2023; Selvaraju et al., 2022; Wagh et al., 2023). The antibacterial activity of LCQDs may be due to the quantum size and functional groups present on the surface. Presumably, interactions between CQDs and bacterial cell membranes directly or electrostatically induce CQDs to penetrate bacterial membranes (Travlou et al., 2018). This could ultimately interfere with the transcription, translation, and replication of bacterial pathogens as well as leakage of cytoplasmic content and protein destruction. In addition, the polyphenol groups can be maintained as surface functional groups even after the carbonization of dried lemon peel. Polyphenols are known for their antimicrobial effectiveness against multidrug-resistant pathogens due to direct killing, attenuation of bacterial pathogenicity, destabilizing cytoplasmic membrane and β-lactamases, and inhibition of topoisomerase (Daglia, 2012; Manso et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2017).

LCQDs exhibited concentration-dependent antioxidant activity when evaluated using ABTS and DPPH assay, as shown in Fig. 2 b. Antioxidant activity using the ABTS method was found to significantly (p < 0.05) increase from 31.5 ± 0.4% to 100% when the LCQD concentration increased from 12.5 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL. In addition, the concentration-dependent increase was confirmed using the DPPH method, but it was lower than the ABTS method (13.7 ± 0.2 to 76.5 ± 0.2). The antioxidant activity of LCQDs can vary greatly depending on the presence, structure, size, and composition of polyphenols, and the results were consistent with other previous reports (Janus et al., 2020; Saleem et al., 2023; Surendran et al., 2021). The use of different solvents could result in different antioxidant potentials since polar solvents release CQDs more readily than non-polar solvents (Nawaz et al., 2020). As an active molecule, phenolic compounds such as flavonoids and phenolic acids have great potential to scavenge free radicals. Recent studies suggest that polyphenols on the surface of CQDs are retained after the carbonization process with high surface area and abundant functionalities (Innocenzi and Stagi, 2023; Moniruzzaman et al., 2022; Otang and Afolayan, 2016). These properties might provide numerous sites for antioxidant reactions, allowing CQDs to efficiently scavenge free radicals to prevent oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Digital images and apparent color of the composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Film transparency can be critical to the visual appearance of films and customer satisfaction. The visual transparency of composite films is given in Fig. 3. The PVA film neat is highly transparent and flexible, but the integration of LCQDs changed the film color to light yellowish brown. This could be due to the uniform distribution of LCQDs throughout the PVA matrix. Even after the integration of LCQDs, the films have shown better transparency. The color variables of the composites are represented in Table 1. Adding LCQDs significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the brightness of the film while improved the redness (a) and yellowness (b) values. The total color difference (ΔE) of the composites was also found to increase with increasing concentration of LCQDs. The L values of the films prepared with 1%, 2%, and 3% LCQDs decreased with increasing weight percentage of LCQDs. In addition, the a and b values increased with higher LCQDs, which could be attributed to the light yellowish color of the LCQDs solution. However, when 3% LCQDs are added, a, b, and ΔE increased whereas the L value decreased. The change in color was previously reported for gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol) based film containing used coffee-based CQDs (Min et al., 2023).

The surface morphology and cross-sectional microstructure of the composite films were analyzed by FE-SEM ( Fig. 4). The neat PVA film and PVA/LCQDs films showed intact morphology without voids and cracks in the surface section of SEM images. The absence of these cracks could be due to the good compatibility between the LCQDs and the PVA matrix. The reason could be the hydrogen bonding among the functional groups present in LCQDs and the polymer chains. Uniform distribution with a smooth surface without cracks was previously reported for CQDs containing composite films based on chitosan and gelatin (Ezati et al., 2022). Similar observations were also reported for gelatin films incorporated with potato skin-based CQDs (Min et al., 2022). Even at higher concentrations, LCQDs are expected to be finely distributed throughout the polymer matrix, improving other functionalities of the composite films.

FTIR spectra confirmed that LCQDs contained abundant hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups ( Fig. 5). The broad absorption band observed between 3500 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1 can be attributed to OH groups (Tyagi et al., 2016). The presence of C-H stretching vibration and CO can be observed at 2922 cm−1 and 1776 cm−1, respectively. The occurrence of the strong peak centered at 1573 cm−1 can be attributed to the unsaturated CC vibrations generated inside the carbon core of LCQDs (Šafranko et al., 2021). The presence of amino groups could be attributed to the peak centered at 1396 cm−1 according to a report by (Ţucureanu et al., 2016). The functional moieties present in the PVA-LCQDs-based films are represented in Fig. 5 . The broad and most abundant functional groups between 3500 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching of hydroxyl groups due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding (Alaş et al., 2022). The peak at 2926 cm−1 could be ascribed to the asymmetric stretching mode of CH2 groups. The new peak around 1746 cm−1 associated with the LCQDs incorporated composite film can be attributed to the stretching vibration of carbonyl group (CO). The bands corresponding to 1080 cm−1 could be due to the hydroxyl C-O stretching whereas 910 and 837 cm−1 represent the C-C and CH2 stretching, respectively (Alaş et al., 2022). The addition of LCQDs into the PVA film didn’t show any additional peak which could be due to the overlapping peaks of additives and the matrix (Ng Hau Kwan et al., 2020). Such absence of novel functional peaks and chemical interactions was previously reported for PVA film incorporated with banana paste-based CQDs (Zhao et al., 2022).

The XRD patterns of LCQDs and PVA-based films are given in Fig. 6. The XRD pattern of LCQDs have shown a broad diffraction peak at 2θ value of 23.3 and could be due to the amorphous structure of dots. Similar pattern was previously reported for citrus based CQDs (Chatzimitakos et al., 2017). For the neat PVA film, peaks around the 2θ value of 19 and 40 could be attributed to the semi-crystalline nature of PVA. The major peaks occurred in crystalline regions arising from strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds among the molecular chains of the PVA matrix (El-Shamy, 2019a; El-Shamy and Zayied, 2020). Additionally, no additional crystalline diffraction peaks were observed for PVA/LCQDs composite films. However, it showed a slight shift in the peak center which is consistent with other studies (Alaş et al., 2022). Therefore, the addition of LCQDs did not change the crystallinity of the films. LCQDs could be dispersed within the PVA matrix without disrupting the crystal structure. Besides this, the homogenous dispersion of LCQDs prevents them from acting as nucleating agents. Hence, the interaction between PVA and LCQDs could be due to the hydrogen bonding that ensures dispersion rather than crystallinity and is consistent with other previous reports (Kuo et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical and water barrier properties of films
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical functions of the PVA composites are given in Table 2 . Neat PVA film has a thickness value of 73 ± 0.005 µm which was slightly increased to 86 ± 0.005 µm in PVA/3% LCQDs composite films. This increase in thickness could be due to increased solid content after the inclusion of LCQDs. The tensile strength of PVA/3% LCQDs composite film also showed a similar observation to that of the thickness. The observed tensile strength of PVA/3% LCQDs film was 92.5 ± 2.1 MPa, which was relatively less compared to that of neat PVA film (84.0 ± 2.6 MPa). The Young’s modulus of the films decreased slightly whereas the EM improved with LCQDs concentrations. Incorporated LCQDs can not only act as reinforcing fillers but can also be uniformly dispersed to improve mechanical strength, stiffness, and toughness. LCQDs can also improve resistance to cracking by acting as a physical barrier to impede crack propagation, thereby improving mechanical strength. In addition, functional groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl) present in them can form hydrogen bonds or intermolecular interactions between the PVA matrix. This could effectively improve the interfacial adhesion between PVA and LCQDs, ultimately improving stress transfer and load-bearing capacity (Dall Agnol et al., 2023; Sani and Alizadeh, 2022). However, PVA-based films have a very high tendency to adsorb moisture from the environment, which can lead to plasticization and reduced mechanical properties.

The water barrier functions of the PVA-based films were analyzed with a WCA analyzer. The hydrophobicity of the PVA film was improved by adding LCQDs. The water contact angle for the neat PVA film was 49.3° and increased to 62.2° for the PVA/3% LCQD composite film ( Fig. 7a). The improvement in the water contact angle of the composite film can be attributed to the compact structure developed between PVA and LCQDs through intra- and inter-molecular hydrogen bonds. A similar observation was previously reported for D-glucose-derived CQDs embedded in PVA films (El-Shamy, 2019b). The slight increase in hydrophobicity may be due to the introduction of functional groups to the film surface. This further alters the surface energy of the PVA-based films, enabling it less prone to interacting with polar solvents. This causes the water molecules to form spheres with limited contact with the film surface, increasing the water contact angle. In this aspect, LCQDs are known to create tortuous pathways that obstruct the diffusion of water molecules into the PVA matrix. Reduced water uptake may also be an effective property to protect the mechanical properties of the PVA matrices. However, the addition of LCQDs has been shown to increase the solubility of the films. The solubility percentage of the neat PVA film was 4.3% and increased to 10.34% for the PVA/3% LCQD composite film (Fig. 7b). The increased solubility percentage of the composites could be due to the surface hydrophilic nature of the LCQDs. These properties are expected to influence the release of active components in the film, which are beneficial for conferring antimicrobial and antioxidant activities (Ezati et al., 2022).

The UV barrier functions of films are very significant as they can extend the shelf life of packed foods through reducing UV light-induced damage. The optical and UV barrier properties of the PVA composite films are provided in Table 3 . PVA/LCQDs composite exhibited prominent concentration-dependent optical properties. The light transmittance of neat PVA film was 89.1 at 660 nm and decreased to 78.2% in the PVA/3% LCQDs composite film. The UV barrier functions of composites were analyzed at 250 nm (UV C), 300 nm (UV B), and 350 nm (UV A). The UV barrier functions of PVA-based composite films were also improved with increasing concentrations of LCQDs. It was found that approximately 99.98% of the light in the UV C region was adsorbed on the PVA/3% LCQDs composite film, whereas only 21.5% was adsorbed on the neat PVA film. Similarly, 99.92% and 99.09% of UV B and UV A were adsorbed by PVA/3% LCQD composite film. However, the neat PVA film adsorbed only 20.7% at UV B and 15.2% at UV A. The efficient UV barrier properties can be attributed to the size-dependent bandgap and quantum confinement effects of LCQDs and the presence of functional moieties. Moreover, the occurrence of aromatic carbon structures, multiple scattering, enhanced interfacial interactions, and surface modification can also improve UV barrier properties (Kumar et al., 2022). The incorporated LCQDs can have a broad absorption spectrum that can prevent UV from penetrating the PVA matrix. In addition, the quantum confinement effect of LCQDs can enhance the absorption and scattering of UV light (Bhattacharya et al., 2015), thus effectively contributing to reduce the photochemical degradation of PVA polymer and food spoilage.

The PVA/LCQDs composite exhibited good blue light emission when stimulated by UV light (365 nm) ( Fig. 8). A possible reason might be that the abundant hydroxyl groups of PVA could form hydrogen bonds efficiently, thereby strengthening the carbon-oxygen bonds in LCQDs, preventing non-radiative relaxation. Studies have shown that hydrogen bonding is important for conferring phosphorescence, which can effectively lock luminescent species and inhibit intermolecular motion (Deng et al., 2013). In addition, the excellent barrier properties of PVA may hinder direct collisions between carbonyls and oxygen that promote phosphorescence (Gaume et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of films
ce:section.ce:para: Oxidative degradation of lipids and other food components can reduce food quality and freshness. Lipid peroxidation can also cause off-flavors, rancidity, discoloration, and loss of nutrients in packaged foods. Food packing materials with antioxidants can extend the shelf life of foods by reducing the rate of oxidation. In particular, polyphenolic compounds such as flavonoids and phenolic acids have great potential as reactive molecules to scavenge free radicals and suitable hydrogen donors (Oun et al., 2023). The antioxidant potential of PVA/LCQDs composites is represented in Fig. 9a. The PVA neat film didn’t show any antioxidant potential but PVA/LCQDs composite films showed a concentration-dependent antioxidant potential. The antioxidant activities of PVA/LCQDs composite films ranged from 33.1% to 98.9%. The antioxidant activity of LCQDs could be highly reliant on the presence of polyphenols, vitamin C, structure, size, and composition. Many studies suggest that polyphenols with high surface area and rich functional groups on the surface of CQDs are retained after the carbonization process. Additionally, some studies have shown that these properties provide numerous active sites for antioxidant reactions, allowing CQDs to scavenge free radicals efficiently (Annu et al., 2021). The results are in good agreement with previous studies based on carbon dot-reinforced PVA films (Alaş et al., 2022). Previously, not only PVA but also carrageenan/alginate films embedded with CQDs were reported to have better antioxidant activity (98.6 ± 0.3%) by ABTS method. Therefore, PVA/LCQD composite films are expected to have great potential in antioxidant packaging applications.

Microbial spoilage is one of the major problems associated with packaged food materials. Active packaging can have great potential to effectively increase shelf life and food quality by inhibiting and limiting the entry of microorganisms. The microbial reduction rate of the PVA-based composite films is given in Fig. 9b. The neat PVA film didn’t show any antimicrobial activity against S. aureus and S. enterica. However, PVA/3% LCQDs composite film showed 100% bactericidal effects against the selected pathogens after 6 h of exposure. PVA/1% LCQDs and PVA/2% LCQDs composite films showed some bacteriostatic effects, but it was negligible when compared to PVA/3% LCQDs composite film. The antimicrobial activity of PVA-based films can be attributed to the successful release of LCQDs from the films. The antimicrobial mechanism of LCQDs was discussed in the previous section on the antimicrobial properties of LCQDs. Previously, CQDs incorporated carboxymethyl cellulose-based functional films were reported to exhibit excellent bactericidal effects, including a 5-log reduction in the number of bacteria (Riahi et al., 2022). Additionally, carrageenan/alginate films containing Allium sativum-derived CQDs were reported to have excellent antimicrobial activity against E. coli and L. monocytogenes (Khan et al., 2023c). Therefore, PVA/3% LCQDs composite film with excellent antibacterial activity can be an efficient material for food packaging applications.

Innovations in nanotechnology, along with eco-friendly approaches, have paved the way for the exploration of natural resources for active packaging applications. In this study, fluorescent and multifunctional active PVA-based composite films containing LCQDs were developed by solvent casting method. LCQDs showed efficient antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, confirming their potential for use as active agents. Microstructural analysis by FE-SEM profiles showed strong compatibility between LCQDs and the PVA matrix. The mechanical and water barrier functions of PVA/3% LCQDs film were slightly enhanced. In addition, PVA/3% LCQDs composite film exhibited 100% UV blocking and antioxidant properties. The antimicrobial assay showed the efficient bactericidal potential of PVA/3% LCQDs composite film against S. aureus and S. enterica. Therefore, the results offer the possibility of using PVA/3% LCQDs composite film as a potential UV-blocking, antioxidant, and antibacterial packaging film for active packaging applications.

Aswathy Jayakumar: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sabarish Radoor: Writing – review & editing. Gye Hwa Shin: Writing – review & editing. Jun Tae Kim: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
